title: Witch Week - Chapter 15 (Fragment 6)
text:
“You fool!” said Inquisitor Littleton. “That was my very latest model witch-finder!” He glared at Chrestomanci. “Right. I’ve had enough of this foul thing. Get it for me.”

The huge man put his gun back in its holster and marched towards Chrestomanci. Nirupam quickly stuck up a long arm. “Please. Just a moment. I think Miss Cadwallader may be a witch too.”

Everyone at once looked at Miss Cadwallader. She said, “How dare you, child!” but she was as white as Mr. Wentworth.

And this, Nan realized, was where she came in. She was not sure how, but she surged to her feet all the same, in such haste that she nearly knocked her desk over like Estelle. Everyone stared at her. Nan felt terrible. For a long, long instant, she went on standing there without a thought in her head and without one scrap of confidence to help her. But she knew she could not just sit down again. She began to talk.

“Just a moment,” she said. “Before you do another thing, I’ve got to tell you about Guy Fawkes. He’s the reason almost everybody in the world is a witch, you know. The main thing about Guy Fawkes is that he was the kind of man who can never do anything right. He meant well, but he was a failure—”

“Make that girl shut up!” Inquisitor Littleton said, in his harsh, bossy voice. Nan looked at him nervously, and then at the two huge men. None of them moved. In fact, now that she looked, everyone seemed to be stuck and frozen exactly where they were when she first stood up. She looked at Chrestomanci. He was staring vaguely into the distance and did not seem to be doing anything either, but Nan was suddenly sure that Chrestomanci was somehow holding everything in one place to give her a chance to explain. That made her feel much better.

She had gone on talking all the time she was looking around, explaining about the Gunpowder Plot, and what a mistake the conspirators made choosing Guy Fawkes to do the blowing up. Now she seemed to be going on to explain about other worlds.

“There were an awful lot of Guy Fawkeses in an awful lot of worlds,” she heard herself saying. “And he was a failure in every one. Some people are like that. There are millions of other worlds, you know. The big differences get made at the big events in history, where a battle gets either won or lost. Both things can’t happen in one world, so a new one splits off and goes different after that. But there are all sorts of smaller things that can go two ways as well, which don’t make a world split off. You’ve probably all had those kinds of dreams that are like your usual life, except that a lot of things are not the same, and you seem to know the future in them. Well, this is because these other worlds where two things can happen spread out from our own world like rainbows, and sort of flow into one another—”

Nan found herself rather admiring this description. She was inspired now. She could have talked for hours. But there was not much point unless she could persuade the rest of 6B to do something. Everyone was just staring at her.

“Now our world should really just be a rainbow-stripe in another proper world,” she said. “But it isn’t. And I’m going to tell you why, so that we can all do something about it. I told you Guy Fawkes was a failure. Well, the trouble was, he knew he was. And that made him very nervous, because he wanted to do at least this one thing right and blow up Parliament properly. He kept going over in his mind all the things that could go wrong: He could be betrayed, or the gunpowder could be damp, or his candle could go out, or his fuse might not light—he thought of all the possibilities, all the things that make the rainbow-stripes of not-quite-different worlds. And in the middle of the night, he got so nervous that he went and lit the fuse, just to make sure it would light. He wasn’t thinking that November 5, the day he was doing it, was the last day of Witch Week, when there is so much magic around in the world that all sorts of peculiar things happen—”

vocabulary.definition: Feeling respect or approval for the way something is described.
vocabulary.example: I’ve had enough of this foul thing.

vocabulary.definition: Very bad or unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: “There were an awful lot of Guy Fawkeses in an awful lot of worlds,” she heard herself saying.

vocabulary.definition: A fight between armed forces.
vocabulary.example: The big differences get made at the big events in history, where a battle gets either won or lost.

vocabulary.definition: The feeling or belief that one can rely on someone or something; firm trust.
vocabulary.example: For a long, long instant, she went on standing there without a thought in her head and without one scrap of confidence to help her.

vocabulary.definition: People who plan secretly to do something unlawful or harmful.
vocabulary.example: She had gone on talking all the time she was looking around, explaining about the Gunpowder Plot, and what a mistake the conspirators made choosing Guy Fawkes to do the blowing up.

vocabulary.definition: Refers to the action or decision taken by conspirators.
vocabulary.example: That made her feel much better.

vocabulary.definition: Lack of success.
vocabulary.example:
He meant well, but he was a failure—”

“Make that girl shut up!

vocabulary.definition: Offensive to the senses, especially through having a disgusting smell or taste or being unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: I’ve had enough of this foul thing.

vocabulary.definition: Unpleasantly rough or jarring to the senses.
vocabulary.example: ” Inquisitor Littleton said, in his harsh, bossy voice.

vocabulary.definition: Excessive speed or urgency of movement or action; hurry.
vocabulary.example: She was not sure how, but she surged to her feet all the same, in such haste that she nearly knocked her desk over like Estelle.

vocabulary.definition: A holder for a pistol or other firearm, typically made of leather and worn on a belt or under the arm.
vocabulary.example:
”

The huge man put his gun back in its holster and marched towards Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: A person making an inquiry, especially one seen as excessively harsh or searching.
vocabulary.example: ” said Inquisitor Littleton.

vocabulary.definition: Of extraordinary quality, as if arising from some external creative impulse.
vocabulary.example: She was inspired now.

vocabulary.definition: The power of apparently influencing events by supernatural forces.
vocabulary.example: He wasn’t thinking that November 5, the day he was doing it, was the last day of Witch Week, when there is so much magic around in the world that all sorts of peculiar things happen—”

vocabulary.definition: The most important point or factor.
vocabulary.example: I’ve had enough of this foul thing.

vocabulary.definition: A particular design or version of a product.
vocabulary.example: “That was my very latest model witch-finder!

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows anxiety or apprehension.
vocabulary.example: Nan looked at him nervously, and then at the two huge men.

vocabulary.definition: Strange or odd; unusual.
vocabulary.example: He wasn’t thinking that November 5, the day he was doing it, was the last day of Witch Week, when there is so much magic around in the world that all sorts of peculiar things happen—”

vocabulary.definition: Cause someone to do something through reasoning or argument.
vocabulary.example: But there was not much point unless she could persuade the rest of 6B to do something.

vocabulary.definition: To convince the other people involved.
vocabulary.example:
”

The huge man put his gun back in its holster and marched towards Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: A thing that may happen or be the case.
vocabulary.example: He kept going over in his mind all the things that could go wrong: He could be betrayed, or the gunpowder could be damp, or his candle could go out, or his fuse might not light—he thought of all the possibilities, all the things that make the rainbow-stripes of not-quite-different worlds.

vocabulary.definition: A real or appropriate version of a world in context.
vocabulary.example: He’s the reason almost everybody in the world is a witch, you know.

vocabulary.definition: To separate or break away from a group or whole.
vocabulary.example: Both things can’t happen in one world, so a new one splits off and goes different after that.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to move or set in place.
vocabulary.example: Nirupam quickly stuck up a long arm.

vocabulary.definition: To move suddenly and powerfully forward or upward.
vocabulary.example: She was not sure how, but she surged to her feet all the same, in such haste that she nearly knocked her desk over like Estelle.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is uncertain, indefinite, or unclear.
vocabulary.example: He was staring vaguely into the distance and did not seem to be doing anything either, but Nan was suddenly sure that Chrestomanci was somehow holding everything in one place to give her a chance to explain.

vocabulary.definition: Continued speaking.
vocabulary.example: For a long, long instant, she went on standing there without a thought in her head and without one scrap of confidence to help her.
